title: Honey-Roast Carrots and Parsnips
subtitle: Two roots together, glazed with honey in the final minutes
excerpt: Carrots and parsnips roast together well because both benefit from high heat and a honey glaze in the final minutes. The difference is that parsnips need par-boiling first to guarantee a tender centre, while carrots can go in raw. Starting the parsnips 10 minutes before the carrots gives both a similar texture by the time the honey goes on.
sourceNotes: Traditional British Sunday roast accompaniment. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: glazed root vegetables alongside the joint. Combined honey-roasted carrot and parsnip is a standard modern British Sunday roast presentation.

content.text: Carrots and parsnips appear together on almost every British Sunday roast plate, but they behave differently in the oven. Parsnips are denser and starchier; without par-boiling first, the interior stays hard while the outside darkens. Carrots have a higher water content and a thinner profile when halved lengthways, so they cook through from raw in the same time as a par-boiled parsnip. Starting the parsnips 10 minutes before the carrots brings both to the same point when the honey glaze goes on.
Bring a large saucepan of salted water to the boil. Add the {{parsnip}} parsnip pieces and cook for 5 minutes. Drain and leave to steam-dry in the colander for 2 minutes.
Roast the parsnips, then add the carrots

Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Toss the parsnips in half the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}. Spread in a single layer in a roasting pan. Roast for 10 minutes. Add the {{carrot}} carrots, toss to coat in the pan juices, and return to the oven for a further 30 to 35 minutes, turning once at the halfway point.

Drizzle {{honey}} of honey over all the vegetables and toss to coat. Scatter the thyme leaves if using. Return to the oven for a final 8 to 10 minutes until the honey has caramelised and the vegetables are sticky and deeply golden. Watch closely: honey burns faster than the vegetables will further colour.

rows.fix: Halve thick carrots lengthways or quarter them to match the parsnip pieces in cross-section. The thinner the piece, the faster it cooks.
rows.cause: Carrots were thicker than the parsnips
rows.symptom: Parsnips done but carrots still firm

rows.fix: Add honey only in the final 8 to 10 minutes. If it catches, reduce the oven by 10°C for the final phase.
rows.cause: Oven too hot or honey added too early
rows.symptom: Honey burning before vegetables are caramelised

content.text: Carrots and parsnips roast well together. Both take the same oven heat. Both take the same glaze. Roasting them in one tin saves hob space on a busy Sunday. It is one tray to manage, not two. The pairing is partly tradition, partly practicality.
